Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Focus on specific, measurable objectives linked to the call expected outcomes. Explain why the project is ambitious and beyond the current state of the art.
Write the Objectives and ambition section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.1 Objectives  
Most plastic packaging has deficient end of life (EoL) options leading to a large burden on the environment and depletion of often used non-renewable resources. Only 31% of plastic is currently recycled while 41% is incinerated and 27% landfilled. Indeed, on the one hand, many food packaging use multilayer structures to meet required performances but those are currently very difficult to recycle. On the other hand, polylactic acid (PLA) is currently the most affordable bio-based and biodegradable plastic but its biodegradability, and that of many other biodegradable biopolymers, is not granted in every environment: PLA is currently only biodegradable under thermophilic conditions in industrial composting or digestion plants at limited thickness. Several companies, such as NOVAMONT, BASF, etc., have developed polymers that can meet home compostability requirements. Nevertheless, further improvements are needed to provide cost-effective solutions with high bio-based contents and suitable performances for demanding packaging applications while still achieving compostability in mild conditions.  
Using sustainably sourced comonomers, additives, and fillers to formulate novel PLA copolymers and compounds, we will deliver recyclable-by-design cost-competitive packaging solutions that can be mechanically recycled or industrially/domestically composted and are even suitable for anaerobic digestion (AD). We will tailor the bioplastic biodegradation through copolymerisation with selected diacids, reactive extrusion (REX) with specific additives that enhance the biodegradation potential, as well as formulation of blends with natural fillers from agro-wastes to speed up disintegration.  
Furthermore, high permeability often limits the application of bioplastics for packing sensitive food products. In order to solve this limitation, we will enhance the barrier properties of the bioplastics using previously developed protein-based coatings, as well as novel fatty acid (FA) grafting (nanometric layers), which, in spite of being applied in multilayer films, can be later removed and do not jeopardize their posterior recyclability or their biodegradability. The hydrophobic grafting will further contribute to reducing product waste at end of packaging use through a release/easy emptying effect.  
Likewise, in the field of textiles, most used coatings (polyamide, polyurethane...) are not bio-based and of different nature than the fibres we apply them on, limiting the properties of the recycled materials. Here, PLA (or our BIOnTOP copolymers) based coatings and FA grafting will be developed, allowing reprocessing of the coated fibres without significant loss of properties. Tuning our BIOnTOP materials/packaging structures, we will respond to end users’ needs for tailored EoL options. To demonstrate the broad potential of our bio-based packaging, we have selected 7 synergistic disposable packaging materials types with very broad markets where current waste management situations have to be improved:  
1. Recyclable (material & organically), home-compostable monomaterial trays & films for fruits and vegetables (F&V).  
2. Recyclable (material & organically) multilayer trays & films compatible with MAP (modified atmosphere packaging) for e.g. dairy and personal care products.  
3. Home compostable & organically recyclable nets for F&V.

1.1 Objectives  
The increasing use of bioplastics in the packaging sector is driven by our continuous demand for sustainable products by consumers and brands alike, due to a growing awareness of the environmental impact and the need to reduce our fossil resources dependency. Our bioplastics industry’s continuous innovations make the number of manufacturers, converters, and end-users increasing steadily. New and innovative biopolymers such as polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) show a very high relative growth rate. With a global production capacity of 25,300 Ton in 2019, we expect an increase up to 160,000 Ton in the next 5 years (from 1.2% to 6.6% of bioplastics’ global production capacity). In this context, new ways of producing PHAs are going to take great importance in the short future, being one of the challenges open to source it from 2nd-3rd generation raw materials instead of raw sugars.  
PHAs are an important polymer family; they are 100 percent bio-based, with versatile biodegradability properties in most environments exceeding those of any other bioplastic, recyclable, and featuring a wide array of physical and mechanical properties depending on their chemical composition, from very flexible poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyvalerate) (PHBV) to rigid polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB), showing similar properties to some fossil-based materials like polypropylene (PP) and polyethylene (PE) and being a good biodegradable and compostable alternative in packaging applications. However, further improvements are needed to provide cost-effective solutions with suitable performances for demanding packaging applications, and alternative environmentally sustainable end of life (EoL) that maximizes the retention of the added value of these biopolymers in a new circular plastic economy.  
On the other hand, spent organic materials have yet to reach an industrial level of market potential related to a value hierarchy (Figure 1). It is the case of brew spent grains (BSG), the most abundant by-product from the brewery industry, available at low or no cost and produced in large quantities. Being high value, its use as a feedstock is limited at present, primarily as animal feed (70%), bioethanol (10%), and landfilled (20%). Main challenges are a high moisture and low fermentable sugar content (mainly C6 sugars), making microbial growth and spoilage possible in only 7–10 days, thus being an environmental problem. Despite these challenges, BSG by-product is one of the most promising candidates as a PHA feedstock, because: i) high amounts are produced in Europe every year and in almost all European countries, ii) it is not seasonal, iii) it is highly stable in comparison with other food by-products, iv) 2G sugars isolation from BSG is more efficient since the complex structure of lignocellulose is damaged during malting and mashing and, therefore, the material is much more prone to enzymatic hydrolysis (less recalcitrant), v) BSG’s proteins act as a nitrogen source avoiding the use of expensive nitrogen supplements in the fermentation process, vi) BSG’s lipids can also be a carbon source for PHA production. Although the brewing industry tends to be more environmentally friendly than other industries, the increasing demand for sustainability in all operations could be satisfied by reducing the amount of BSG still landfilled or used in low added value applications. Our BioSupPack strategy and innovations, shown in Figure 2, have been well defined to overcome the described limitations and deliver novel packaging that will respond to end users’ needs and European sustainability policies.  
In terms of packaging performance, we have selected PHA for the production of novel BioSupPack packaging due to its good mechanical performance in rigid packaging, its ability to be applied as a wet coating on paper/cardboard substrates, and its better gas, fat, and liquid barrier properties compared to other bioplastics like polylactic acid (PLA) and even some fossil-based materials. Other requirements like squeezable properties and suitability for hot filling and microwavable applications are also addressed. The following approaches will lead to high-performing bio-based packaging: For primary packaging: i) production of tailored PHA compounds for rigid packaging for food, cosmetic, and homecare products; ii) use of already developed plasma technology to improve barrier performance of food and cosmetic packaging, mainly in terms of oxygen barrier, avoiding the use of multi-layered packaging structures and facilitating their posterior EoL; iii) optimization of recently developed patent-pending PHA based wet coatings to protect rigid service packaging based on paper pulp/cardboard from moisture, liquids, and fats; iv) grafting of fibre-based PHA coated substrates to protect food packaging from gases, fat, and liquid; v) production of PHA based compounds suitable for squeezable packaging for food and cosmetics products. For beverage secondary packaging, specifically beer glass bottles multipack, we will produce a PHA based compound for rigid clips that will replace current packages (rustic wood packaging or plastic rings to carry six-bottle packs).  
In terms of biobased packaging materials from biomass feedstock, we will valorise BSG to produce PHAs, specifically PHB. The whole process contemplates innovations in upstream BSG pre-treatment, fermentation, and downstream PHB separation and purification stages focusing on the development of an economically feasible, highly efficient, and sustainable bioprocess: i) enhancement of BSG to sugar conversion yield using the already developed plasma technology towards lignocellulose pre-treatment at low temperature combined with sequential enzymatic hydrolysis of cellulose and hemicellulose. Lipid and protein hydrolysis will also be assessed to supply carbon and nitrogen sources to the fermentation stage; ii) PHB production via fermentation with conventional mesophiles and novel extremophilic bacteria for reduced risk of contamination in non-aseptic cultures; iii) combined use of sugars and 3-hydroxybutyric acid (3HB) monomer derived via enzymatic recycling of used PHA packages as carbon sources in fermentation; iv) extraction of PHB from bacterial biomass by ‘green’ solvent and recyclable switchable anionic surfactants to facilitate PHB extraction of high purity and yield, avoiding any PHB degradation leading to retention of the molecular weight (Mw) produced during fermentation. The PHB obtained will be used for the development of tailored compounds (physical modifications by blending with necessary additives, and commercial PHAs if required) for rigid packaging for food, cosmetics, homecare, and beverage. Physical modifications of selected commercial PHA grades aimed at wet coatings for food pulp based rigid packaging (service packaging) will address the need for alternatives to PE coated paper that cannot be recycled nor degraded in nature.  
In terms of packaging EoL sustainability, the inherent properties of PHA will be exploited not only in the design and production of packaging prototypes, but also in the selected EoL by enzymatic recycling. Enzymatic degradation of PHB is easier and faster compared to other biopolyesters like PLA or polybutylene succinate. This EoL will provide profitable precursors to be reintroduced in the value chain, maximizing the retention of the biopolymer added value in a new circular plastic economy. Specifically, derived 3HB will be an additional carbon in fermentation for PHB production, besides BSG’s sugars and lipids, allowing us to produce the original biopolymer with the same properties. Whereas compostability can be suited for applications whose mechanical recycling is extremely difficult or impossible, e.g. packaging waste containing remains of the product packed or PE coated paper as PE cannot be separated from the paper fraction making the packaging not recyclable, enzymatic recycling using selective enzymes prone to hydrolyse PHAs (vs. product remains, paper) is a very suitable EoL. While considering recycling as a favourable option for PHA upcycling, it may require adaptation of the sorting lines. As such, photonic based solutions will be delivered to assess the correct sorting towards industrial recycling streams. The proposed EoL will contemplate: i) use of plasma as pre-treatment of the packaging waste, reducing the polymer Mw and modifying its surface properties to facilitate subsequent enzymatic hydrolysis; ii) exploit known microorganisms producing PHB depolymerase releasing monomer 3HB during PHB hydrolysis; iii) select organisms from currently available metagenomic databases created from extreme environments that produce PHB depolymerase with higher 3HB production rate than existing ones; iv) implement enzyme engineering to improve PHB depolymerisation efficiency to 3HB; v) employ cloning and heterologous enzyme expression to overproduce the targeted PHB depolymerase; vi) optimise enzyme cocktails to maximise PHB depolymerisation efficiency. Our BioSupPack also considers the mechanical recycling of industrial PHA scraps to be reintroduced in the conversion processes loop, increasing circularity and making the whole process more cost-efficient and environmentally sustainable. For PHA based coated paper, we will address the need for enzymes and bacteria during the repulping of such packaging materials, so that they can be recycled with the simple addition of specific microorganisms or enzymes to the recycling process.  
The project contemplates the production of different rigid packaging prototypes representing large volumes in the packaging market, and where our current performance of biobased biodegradable materials, waste management, and EoL contexts must be improved (Figure 3). It is addressed to applications where our novel packaging can influence consumers’ perception about the use of plastic packaging. The innovative materials, which will replace conventional polyolefins like PE and PP, will cover the food, beverage, cosmetic, and homecare packaging of early adopters present in our consortium, but their relevance goes far beyond this, with potential

1.1 Objectives  
The last decade has experienced a strong increase in technological developments for improved material functionality. One of these involves surface treatments, which employ complex (multilayer) structures designed to provide multifunctionality in a single solution. Within this text, for the sake of simplicity, we will refer to these complex multilayer surface treatments as ‘coatings’. This definition includes functional and performance coatings on textiles, paints on plastics, foil lamination, and adhesion layers for plastics parts (polymer-polymer and polymer-metal connections). These ‘coatings’ have a wide range of advantages and benefits but currently cannot be recycled because of their hybrid nature.  
The main goal of our project is to enable the circular use of coated parts by developing novel smart polymer material systems, and their corresponding recycling processes, currently a missing link – see figure. We will demonstrate our project results via concrete use-cases for outdoor gear, household electronics, and automotive parts. A systemic approach for delivering breakthroughs all along the value chain will be followed, see figure.  
To reach this ambitious goal and to maximise the impact of our DECOAT project on the textile and plastics sector, we define the following concrete key objectives (KO): 
- KO1. Development of a toolbox of triggerable materials for debonding on demand suitable for use on textiles and plastics. Result: ‘coatings’1 that can be removed by various triggers, e.g. thermal via infra-red, heat or steam (targeted temperatures 100-150°C), chemical treatment or ‘microwaves’2. 
- KO2. Development and design of triggerable debonding coating solutions for ‘disassembly’ of parts or objects and for ‘removal’ of the coating from the multilayer structure. Focus is on acrylates, polyurethanes, and PVC materials. Result: generic disassembly and removal solutions for punctual connections (0D), linear joints (1D), and planar coatings (2D). 
- KO3. Automation of sorting according to the required trigger. Result: pilot line enabling us to sort the coated textiles and plastics based on the separation trigger incorporated.  
- KO4. Integration of debonding systems in a continuous pilot plant recycling line for coated parts. Result: pilot line enabling the recyclability of coated parts (flexible and rigid). The system will integrate the following elements: sorting and grinding, the novel debonding system, mechanical recycling, and melt extrusion, all steps running in a continuous way. 
- KO5. Demonstration via use cases (outdoor gear, household electronics, and automotive parts) using the pilot recycling plant and the adapted coating processes during manufacturing. Result: circularity demo of the use case products. Technology and processing for the use cases brought to TRL 5, accompanied by a plan for upscaling & industrialisation.  
 
1 ‘coatings’ refers here to coatings, paints, and adhesives as defined above and as will be used for simplicity throughout this whole document 
2 ‘microwaves’ will be the most likely option (although it could also be another form of electromagnetic heating like Rf heating -see also section 1.4.1) and as such will be used for simplicity throughout this whole document 

Smart plastic materials with intrinsic recycling properties by design

- KO6. Assessment of environmental impact and implementation of safety-by-design (safe product, safe production, and safe use and recycling). Result: full LCA study for the debonding concepts; reduced uncertainties and risks for human and environmental safety of our DECOAT solutions.  
- KO7. Exploitation and business plans for the DECOAT results, including for the use cases manufacturing guidelines to ensure implementation at industrial scale. Further, for the more generic results (design-material concepts) a thorough analysis will be done for wide scope exploitation within the textile and plastics sectors. Result: tailored business plans for our DECOAT (company) partners, public research roadmap, and a strategy to further exploit and develop the generic DECOAT results, as a basis for implementing the market-creating potential of the DECOAT approach.  
- KO8. Dissemination and communication among relevant European communities. The main target is the European materials and recycling community, supplemented by the wider stakeholders group, both for getting extra feedback during project implementation and for promoting our results. Result: raise awareness, engage for collaboration, and adaptation/integration of our project developments in applications beyond those covered within DECOAT.

The main objective of our project is to develop and promote the adoption of collaborative production engineering approaches (Open Innovation B2B/B2C digital platform and fablabs) in the FMCG sector. This will spur the rapid production and commercialisation of new/innovative personalised or customised soft matter FMCG for the benefit of consumers. KPIs: Feedback from up to 20 SMEs and stakeholders using the platform; >10 new customized products identified.  
SO2: Development of a knowledge base (KB) and open innovation technologies: Our success will be achieved by:  
a) Developing a data management plan (DMP) enabling the end to end use of disparate collated data, allowing different actors to interoperate seamlessly.  
b) Designing an evolving knowledge base; capable of easily representing information including the needs, user experience and the data for a given customised formulated product.  
c) Developing and integrating a Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) and Parametric Life Cost Calculation Analysis (LCCA) models to support transparent analysis of ingredient and final product features.  
d) Plugging in, developing and adapting relevant soft matter modelling and simulation tools to digitally explore the performance of new formulations.  
e) Developing and plugging in a digital twin of small-scale formulation process machine (fablab)  
f) Defining common security and privacy requirements based on European monitoring frameworks to allow the collection and analyses of private data.  
g) Evaluating the proposed solution in terms of the execution requirements.  
KPIs: 1 application built on micro services or other API based services, at least 2 applied Machine Learning algorithms, soft matter modelling and simulation tools, LCA/LCCA algorithms, fablab digital twin, performance testing report for all digital components.  
SO3: Ensuring secure open data exchange and cybersecurity: This SO aims to secure the data exchange and share skills between designers, makers, users, communities, manufacturers and other actors in an effective and transparent way on the DIY4U platform. Distributed ledger technology (DLT)/blockchain will be deployed to enhance data sharing and transparency, and to track liability and ownership. It is essential to strengthen our ability to detect, mitigate and recover from risks and cyberattacks to enable a secure operational and tactical DIY4U infrastructure.  
KPIs: 3 partners securely transferring data via the platform, DLT consensus mechanisms auto detecting and responding to cyberattacks attempting to amend records and submit false data.  
SO4: Development of Manufacturing Demonstration Facilities (MDF) and DIY4U Training: After analysing user demands and needs, initial small-scale manufacturing trials of the early personalised/customized formulations designed on the platform will be produced and characterized at MDFs (STEF and CPI) using existing high-throughput and other complex liquid/particle processing equipment. Learning from these trial runs will feed into the requirements specification and design of fablab machines for automated production of powdered and liquid FMCG. Subsequently, we will demonstrate the feasibility of DIY4U manufacturing by building 2 fablab prototypes (1 powdered and 1 liquid) and using them to provide open access collaborative production. These prototypes will be transitioned to commercial design and replicated after our project’s completion. To ensure smooth adoption by consumers, SME users and wider industry, our platform and fablab training will be developed and delivered to stakeholders including future EU industry workforce via dedicated workshops and apprenticeships at MDFs.  
KPIs: 2 Fablab prototypes, Demonstration Reports covering >100 products to check the operability and >10 products to check fablab performance in delivering critical quality attributes. DIY4U Training manuals, At least 4 training sessions delivered to >100 attendees/non-consortium stakeholders, at least 10 students/apprentice trained on DIY4U.  
SO5: Empowering different actors to be more involved in the DIY growth and increasing the DIY product acceptance: We will increase the involvement of makers, customers and stakeholders by:  
a) Enhancing public and end-user awareness on the DIY creativity aspects by providing modern tools, such as serious games and interactive tools on both the digital platform and fablabs.  
b) Identifying and understanding the needs, behaviours and demands of consumers, makers and manufacturers through data collation via cloud-based tools.

Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Focus on specific, measurable objectives linked to the call expected outcomes. Write the Objectives and ambition section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.1 Objectives and ambition  
Access to safe, qualitative, and effective pharmaceutical treatments must remain fully available to us and our animals. This comes to light during the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic probably more than ever, including the need for sustainable supply chains and consumption patterns. At the same time, we must avoid that there is an undue impact of pharmaceutical residues on the environment. Indeed, as highlighted by the pandemic, our health and well-being strongly depend on a healthy environment.  

Boosting the reduction of the environmental impact of pharmaceutical products throughout their entire life cycle. ETERNAL (HORIZON-HLTH-2021-IND-07-01) Page 2 of 45  

In view of an expected increase in medicine consumption in Europe (and indeed worldwide) due to rising demand from ageing populations and a general growing dependence on pharmaceutical drugs, pharmaceuticals are a priority area from an environmental health perspective. One area of growing concern stems from the presence of pharmaceutical residues that can enter the environment at all stages of their life cycle (via effluents from manufacturing, natural excretion, or improper disposal), and can accumulate in fish, vegetables, livestock, and end up in drinking water. Indeed, more than 770 pharmaceuticals and their metabolites have been found in the environment worldwide and there is concern about the potential consequential harm to human health and the environment. Alarm was raised several years ago over the unexpectedly lethal effect of the painkiller diclofenac on vultures in Asia, which were exposed to it via the carcasses of cattle treated with it. More recently, the unexpected presence of nitrosamine impurities in some medicines has highlighted the importance of a sound system for detecting quality problems and of compliance management. Nitrosamines are chemical compounds classified as probable human carcinogens on the basis of animal studies. On 11 March 2019, we adopted a strategic approach to pharmaceuticals in the environment (as required by Article 8c of Directive 2008/105/EC as amended by Directive 2013/39/EU). The approach covers all phases of the lifecycle of pharmaceuticals, from design and production through to use and disposal.  

To address such problems and abate these concerns, our project aims to:  
a) boost the reduction of the environmental impact of pharmaceutical products by advancing a roadmap of technological innovations in the fields of biocatalysts, carcinogenic impurity capture, substitution of non-recycled solvents, correct treatment of recycled solvents, membrane technology, continuous manufacturing processing, and digitalization towards a) green production methods and b) a one-step disposal where drugs are fully metabolised in the body and break down immediately and harmlessly in the environment. This work will be performed in an industry-research-compliance co-design space that addresses the pharmaceutical industry (including SME) needs from the outset, to ensure that we scale up deployable technical solutions and relevant data, and that also integrates the regulatory perspective.  
b) increase the understanding of the environmental impact and toxicity of pharmaceuticals among industry, the research community, and regulators to inform pharmaceutical strategies and policies based on scientific evidence. A key feature will be a holistic full systems approach, considering the full life cycle impact, from manufacture onwards (also considering such aspects as plant cleaning and the entry of cleaning solvents into the environment, supply chain). Thus, among industry, academia, and risk assessors, we will highlight the need to assess the environmental impact of pharmaceuticals from a whole life cycle perspective (including for example, the risks of other chemicals and byproducts at the production stage, and end-of-life considerations).  
c) create an opportunity to catalyse behavioural change or social innovation by:  
i. informing consumers/patients about safe disposal methods for unused or expired medicines. Whilst pharmaceutical residues can enter the environment during the production, consumption, and disposal, incorrect disposal of household pharmaceutical waste is considered the second major pathway into the environment; European citizens do not know how to dispose of unwanted medicines safely, their common disposal practices being toilet, sink, and household rubbish.  
ii. promoting the choosing of the least environmentally-hazardous medicines among healthcare practitioners, as well as sensitizing around the area of improved prescription accuracy (according to the data, 30-40% of antibiotics are misused or wrongly diagnosed), which ensures we also integrate the public health perspective.  

Within this framework, Table 1 outlines the measurable and verifiable objectives of our project:  

Table 1- Measurable and verifiable objectives of the ETERNAL project  
Objective 1: To launch a co-creation lab to ensure the mobilization of an industry-research-compliance partnership that facilitates co-design in an application-industry oriented R&D initiative  
- Scoping sessions between our research-technological development teams and the pharmaceutical industry hosts of the 6 Case Studies to co-design the application-oriented developments (building on the initial Case Study descriptions outlined in Section 3.1b)  
- Definition of the specifications, industry requirements, business/environmental impact KPIs, and regulatory compliance requirements for each of the Case Studies to inform our R&D (TRL3-4) and further scale up (TRL5-6) of our green chemistry, mechanochemistry, and digitalization innovations (WP1, WP2, WP3).  

Objective 2: To reduce the use of solvents and increase the use of green solvents (Final TRL 5-6)  
- Reduction in solvent use thanks to the inclusion of continuous technologies and new synthetic routes (WP1/WP3). The objective will be validated in case studies of MyB, RJF, QS. The reduction of solvent is linked directly to the technology applied and its complexity. We expect a reduction between 20-40% considering each case study. The reduction will be confirmed by comparing the new process with traditional ones.  
- Application of greener solvents (biobased solvents and ionic liquids, e.g. ethyl lactate and others) in new synthesis routes applied by pharmaceutical companies (WP1/WP3): case studies of RJF, MyB, and QS. In the case of solvent substitution, we expect to replace 100% of the solvent used by a greener or biobased alternative.  

Objective 3: To optimize solvent recycle/recovery options for processes with potential carcinogenic impurities like nitrosamines. (TRL5-6)  
- This objective is linked to WP1/WP3 and specifically to case studies of AZ, Angelini Pharma.  
- Application of membrane technology and other technologies for solvent swaps, purification, and product recovery resulting in lower energy consumption. Increase of 15-25% of recycled isobutyl, and up to 90% in the case of Astra Zeneca solvent.  

Objective 4: To apply mechanochemistry in the production of pharmaceutical products (HME) (TRL5-6)  
- Linked to WP1/WP3. Angelini Pharma's case study.  
- Application of Hot Melt Extrusion in the obtaining of pharmaceutical formulations for immediate or modified release- ADS (amorphous solid dispersions) and CSD (crystalline solid dispersions). The validation of this objective will be provided after the analysis of the product quality (comparison with batch process).  

Objective 5: To develop more eco-efficient purification/capture routes (for solvents and wastewater) with biobased products (TRL3-4)  
- Linked to WP1/WP3. Case studies: JRF, Angelini Pharma, AZ, QS  
- Enzymatic degradation of molecules at their end-of-life (waste effluents)  
- Capture of chitosan. Validated by analysis of intermediate of final products along the process line.  

Objective 6: To develop innovative workflow digitalization, Process Analytical Technology (PAT), and Digital Twin solutions to enable Quality by Design and Continuous Manufacturing for competitive and scalable methods of production

1.1 Objectives and ambition  
1.1.1 FreeMe objectives  
Our industrial use of plastics worldwide has increased remarkably over the last 30 years by virtue of their exceptional properties such as low density, lightweight, low cost, versatility, flexibility, strength, and easy machinability. Many plastic parts require a protective metallic coating to enhance their wear and corrosion resistance and to provide a shiny, polished, aesthetically pleasing final surface. The electrodeposition of metals on solid substrates (electroplating) is widely considered the most efficient strategy for producing metal coatings. However, plating on plastics (PoP) via the application of an external electric potential is practically not feasible because most plastics are non-conductive. PoP of common, non-conductive plastics (polymers) such as ABS, PET, PLA, Nylon blends, and 3D printed resins requires the initial deposition of a thin metal film (metallization) via a process called “electroless plating” [1] during which metal cations are chemically reduced to metal inside a liquid bath. Therefore, metal deposition on the polymer surface proceeds in the absence of an external source of electrical power. Importantly, the surface of the polymeric substrate must undergo a specific pre-treatment before the metallization step that includes two crucial steps:  
• etching to increase the hydrophilicity and roughness of the surface that will have a vital role in the adhesion of the metallic film on plastic  
• surface activation to “seed” the surface with a catalyst which in turn creates nucleation sites for the final electroless metallization step.  
Substrate pre-treatment is key to obtaining a homogeneous and dense metal coating during the metallization step. Surface etching enhances metal adhesion whereas surface activation provides finely dispersed nucleation sites for ensuing electroless metal deposition. Currently, our industrial PoP processes utilize Cr6+ based solutions (e.g., chromic acid) for surface etching. With respect to surface activation, Sn-Pd colloidal baths are currently employed. Palladium is an exceedingly rare, precious metal which significantly raises costs. Concluding, our current industrial processes for PoP present the following significant disadvantages in respect to environmental sustainability, occupational safety, and quality of coatings: [2]  

a. A mixture of Chromic (Cr6+) and sulfuric acids bath solution is used as the SoA process for plastic etching. The bath should operate at a temperature between 50-70oC making the process of high risk in relation to occupational safety and environment as Cr6+ is a recognized carcinogenic and highly toxic compound.  
b. Palladium (in solutions with tin salts) is used to catalyze the reduction of copper or nickel to form a continuous conductive layer. This layer is necessary for the deposition of the final electroplated coating on the surface of the plastic component. Pd is a Critical Raw material (CRM) as its use is strongly dependent on imports (i.e. from Russia). Replacement of Pd from processes with more abundant materials is highly desirable.  
c. The plating result is highly dependent on the composition of the plastic component. Inhomogeneities in the final coating or bad adhesion may occur, degrading the aesthetics as well as its functionalities.

Our FreeMe project will investigate two revolutionary approaches for the metallization of polymeric (plastic or resins) surfaces, alternative to the SoA, without the use of hexavalent chromium and palladium.  
The first approach is based on the use of sprayable resins (e.g. epoxy-acrylate resins) with the addition of suitable nickel-based precursors (i.e. anhydrous nickel salts). This composite resin can be deposited uniformly on the surface of the plastic object by spray deposition methods. The nickel precursors are afterwards reduced utilizing appropriate reduction agents creating the necessary nuclei for the next step of electroless deposition and the formation of the conductive layer. The second technology is based on a Cr6+ & Pd free pre-treatment of the polymer surface followed by incorporation of nickel active sites via reduction of nickel salts on the surface of the polymer. Similarly, to the first technology, the electroless step follows. For both cases, the final step is to apply the desired metal coating for the final metal finishing (i.e. nickel, copper, chromium, or even precious metals). The proposed methodologies are based on results of four partners (Gaser, PoliMi, AUTH, and Cnano) obtained by in-house, national, and EC funded research projects. Our FreeMe project main objectives are:  

Elimination of the use of Cr6+ and Pd from the PoP process. Our FreeMe technologies will be based on REACH compliant chemicals, avoiding toxic compounds, and thus ensuring the long-term sustainability of the process.  

To increase the maturity of the proposed FreeMe technology to TRL 5 and bring it closer to market applications, the following specific objectives should be achieved.  
1. Deployment and implementation of a safe and sustainable by design (SSBD) strategy concerning the development of the new FreeMe technologies for PoP that will cover the whole lifecycle (from raw materials to recycling of the plated component). This will be based on the selection of REACH compliant chemicals, evaluation of processes and materials related to safety in the operational environment, end application, and end-of-life scenarios (e.g. recycling) and assessment of the overall sustainability of the technology.  
2. Development of a metallization technology of plastic surfaces (i.e. ABS, PET, PLA, Nylon blends, and 3D printed resins) with a technology based on green REACH compliant sprayable UV-curable composite bio-based resins: Sprayable bio-based epoxy or epoxy-acrylate resins containing anhydrous nickel salts, i.e. NiCl2 particles with size lower than 1.5μm, in concentrations of 0.5-2%wt will be produced. The Ni salt particles in the resin’s surface will be chemically reduced forming Ni nuclei, acting as catalysts for the following electroless process.  
3. Development of Cr6+ free etching technologies for plastics (i.e. ABS, PET, PLA, Nylon blends, and 3D printed resins) pre-treatment. The technology is based on the use of acids or alkali mixtures to micro-etch the surface and prepare it for the activation steps utilizing Ni-based salts and reduction of them. For the 3D printed resins, we will use mixtures of alkali to micro-etch the surface, followed by the activation step.  
For both objectives 2 and 3, the ultimate target is to achieve excellent adhesion of the coatings on the plastic surface (4.9 N/25mm) based on peel tests.  
4. Development of in-silico techniques (simulation and modelling) facilitating both the development and the synthesis and application of the sprayable composite resins and on the optimization of plastic surface etching based on multi-scale models, ranging from atomic to mesoscopic scales.  
5. Development of a fast and accurate inspection method based on spectroscopic imaging ellipsometry and optical coherence tomography for micro-scale monitoring (up to 3 μm) for the QC of the process (KPI: to monitor up to 3μm scale resolution, with 99% probability of defect detection).

Our main goal of GREEN-LOOP is to develop and demonstrate a comprehensive set of novel, cost-effective, and sustainable (green) bio-based products, able to substitute traditional materials. We will implement three value chains:  
i) Bio-rubber: Multifunctional rubber panels (with lignin additives from kraft process residues) with fire retardant and vibrational properties for civil engineering construction.  
ii) Bio-plastic: Bottle closures created from agro-industry residues for the packaging, food, and beverage sectors.  
iii) Wood composites: Sliding bearings for appliance and tools sectors.  
These new bio-based products aim for better mechanical, physical, and chemical properties compared with conventional materials. At the same time, we will ensure a substantially lower environmental footprint, being non-toxic and demonstrating recyclability/reusability/refurbishability. Furthermore, we will master, deploy, and secure autonomy in the strategic value chains developed within our project. European manufacturing is at the center of a twin ecological and digital transition. The integration of the most disruptive technological techniques in Artificial Intelligence, the use of industrial data, the transformation into a circular economy, and the need for flexibility and adaptation to change set the path of the main objectives of the Made in Europe Plan. Also, intelligent and logistic technologies such as A.I. self-learning algorithms will be developed. Furthermore, we will carry out models and simulations to fully understand the different chemical processes, material configurations, and physical behavior. Finally, to boost the Blue Economy, we will pay special attention to conserving the oceans, seas, and marine resources for sustainable development. Due to the circularity by design concept implemented in our project, we will prevent litter from finding its way into the sea in the first place. Our activities are aligned with the EU action plan on zero pollution, offering an opportunity to step up action on contaminants and litter (primarily made up of plastics). This is important since the removal of litter into the seas has been accepted as a risk to the environment and the undertaking of human activities as established in The EU Blue Economy report of 2021.

European manufacturing is at the center of a twin ecological and digital transition. The integration of the most disruptive technological techniques in Artificial Intelligence, the use of industrial data, the transformation into a circular economy, and the need for flexibility and adaptation to change set the path of the main objectives of the Made in Europe Plan. All of this, combined with the increasing threat from Asian and American economies, suggests that more action is needed. Since green, flexible, and advanced manufacture is vital for competitiveness, which we will only obtain using high throughput manufacturing techniques, our bio-based products will contribute to the specific objectives of the MADE IN EUROPE Roadmap 2021-2027. In this context, we will demonstrate three use cases for transitions towards green manufacturing technologies, incorporating adapted and novel manufacturing techniques to produce bio-based materials. Thus, we will contribute to the reduction of the footprint across the whole manufacturing and products lifecycle.  

To sum up, it is essential to remark that we will be setting advanced electric technologies (Microwaves and Ultrasounds) to support manufacturing activities. Also, intelligent and logistic technologies such as A.I. self-learning algorithms will be developed. Furthermore, we will carry out models and simulations to fully understand the different chemical processes, material configurations, and physical behavior. Finally, to boost the Blue Economy, we will pay special attention to conserving the oceans, seas, and marine resources for sustainable development. Due to the circularity by design concept implemented in our project, we will prevent litter from finding its way into the sea in the first place. Our activities are aligned with the EU action plan on zero pollution, offering an opportunity to step up action on contaminants and litter (primarily made up of plastics). This is important since the removal of litter into the seas has been accepted as a risk to the environment and the undertaking of human activities, as established in The EU Blue Economy report of 2021.

1.1 Objectives and ambition  
INPERSO is a holistic deep renovation programme addressing our building’s entire lifecycle and combining industrialisation and personalisation. We will deliver inclusive, affordable, efficient, and sustainable renovation, adaptable to various climate zones and building typologies, but focused on residential and heritage buildings. INPERSO is designed to address the lifecycle of our building, from project design to the end-of-life, addressing the challenges of digitalisation, fragmentation, quality, efficiency and rapidity, while adopting a human-centric approach. This will be reflected in the Integrated Results (IR) of our project, that comprise innovations targeted to address the needs of the sector:  
Digitalisation: The slow pace of digitalisation, the lack of unified resources traversing our building’s life cycle and the time/effort requirements for data collection in pre-renovation buildings will be addressed through the integration of AI and AR-based solutions in innovative products in RE SUITE integrated platform for renovation management (IR1). The platform will provide all the tools needed by us, owners, managers, and contractors to manage the building throughout its lifecycle, addressing inefficiencies and generating significant savings.  
Fragmentation: The renovation and construction value chain is highly fragmented, user feedback is not an integral part of the process, renovation is capital-intensive, disruptive, noisy, and polluting and often does not meet user expectations. In INPERSO, Low-Carbon Renovation Models (IR2 – LCRM) will introduce a human-centric approach to deciding and designing renovation projects, that minimise the performance gap and result in user satisfaction and sustainable behaviour.  
Quality, productivity and safety: Reducing the amount of CDW generated, increasing productivity, up- and re-skilling our workforce, improving workplace conditions and reducing the embodied CO2 of buildings throughout their lifecycle will be achieved through innovative products and processes: the INPERSO Retrofit – Kit (IR3), comprising active/passive envelope systems, renewable energy generation and ventilative HVAC solutions and a vertical Façade 3D Printer (IR4), enabling rapid and efficient retrofit, minimising reworks.  
Efficiency: Achieving and retaining building performance (both in terms of energy and comfort) is a crucial step to increase trust towards renovation. The Energy & Comfort Optimisation Interface (ECO-I, IR5) enables combining personalised renovation according to user needs and providing smart systems to optimize energy demand, especially in warmer climates, and areas with significant natural ventilation and cooling potential.  
Rapidity: To achieve the 2030 and ultimately 2050 climate goals, solutions need to be deployed rapidly and at significant scale, presenting replication and transferability potential while also adapting to current climate changes. The INPERSO completed system (IR6) will be demonstrated and completed to deliver a near-market solution.  

INPERSO is a solution to the multifaceted deep renovation challenge addressing the individual needs of key stakeholders involved in the retrofit process. For building owners & managers, INPERSO will provide a service encompassing the entire lifecycle of the building, from supporting decision making (when, what to renovate, how much to spend), making us an integral part of the process and ensuring that the results reflect our expectations. For stakeholders in the B&C sector (industry and research organisations), we will deliver services and products rendering the renovation process more cost, resource, and time efficient, more sustainable, reducing the embodied CO2 in buildings and improving the quality of projects. For the European citizen, building user or neighbour, our project will make deep renovation more impactful and accessible.

1.1 Objectives Our project’s aim is to provide a portable solution for flexible farm-to-fork sensing of microbial and chemical contamination in food products and along the food production chain. We will develop an integrated solution that combines innovations in mid-infrared (MIR) sensing with smart paper-based sample treatment and advanced data analysis. Our solution will be developed for the detection of microbial and chemical contamination, validated and demonstrated in real scenarios along the food value chain (Fig. 1). Novel light emitting diode (LED) light sources, as well as interband and quantum cascade lasers (ICL/QCL), will be combined with sophisticated waveguide technology to transform MIR sensing from existing lab solutions into a portable solution. The combination of MIR sensing with a paper microfluidic sample handling device is especially powerful, as it provides an easy and affordable means to measure purified extracts of contaminants. Purified extracts are necessary if we want to achieve quantitative spectroscopic determination of levels of chemical and microbial contamination in food matrices at relevant detection limits. We will develop and demonstrate a photonic based monitoring solution for highly important contamination problems in the food production chain, namely (i) fungal and water mould (oomycetes) contamination and mycotoxins in wheat, nuts, dried fruits and aquaponic-based herbs, and (ii) pesticides and antibiotics in aquaponic-based herbs. Hence, the applicability of the developed portable devices will be demonstrated on-site at the end user level (farms and retail), including aquaponics as a novel type of food production system. We will demonstrate that our project solution is highly flexible and can be easily diversified: it can be used at the most critical control points in the production and distribution of these products, Figure 1: Overview of our project. (a) The use of the MI-FI device for daily monitoring, and (b) the HI-FI device for reference analysis along the value chain. Compared to existing testing off-site, considerably more data will be collected. (c) The transfer of the data to platforms such as Food Trust™ and data integration will define a new paradigm for food safety control and reduction of food waste, and thereby represent a disruptive technology in agronomy and food industries. (d) Our devices will feature two different types of photonic technologies (IC-LED and ICL/QCL), and (e) a paper-based microfluidic sample handling unit, for detection of minute concentrations of contaminants. We will address such as the entry point of the European Union for products produced outside the EU, at the farm level (traditional farming, novel forms of farming and on-farm vending), at the distributor level, and the level of the retailer. Our solution is based on two innovative photonic devices that are suitable for a wide range of application scenarios. Both photonic devices will have integrated low-cost paper-based microfluidics for sample collection, pretreatment, and analyte preconcentration, as well as an integrated data analysis platform (see Fig. 1). The first photonic device will be low-cost and handheld, delivering medium-fidelity data (MI-FI device see Fig. 1) by incorporation of innovative MIR light emitting diode (LED) light sources based on interband cascade (IC) technology. The second photonic device will be medium-cost and portable, delivering high-fidelity data (HI-FI device, see Fig. 1) by incorporation of interband and quantum cascade lasers (ICL/QCL). The HI-FI device will provide high-fidelity molecular signatures that can be used for reference measurements. The handheld MI-FI device is tailored for daily monitoring along the whole food chain and even retail, which is feasible due to its low envisioned price. The HI-FI spectra will allow the quantification of contaminations at relevant detection limits and will provide sufficient precision for accreditation of the method. Advanced data analysis will be used to calibrate the MI-FI and HI-FI spectral data. The obtained data will be further utilized by transferring it to and integrating it into platforms such as the blockchain-based IBM Food Trust™ data platform for tracing food contamination and improving food quality. The availability of big data on food safety and the integration of the data into food blockchain platforms will define a new paradigm for food safety control and waste reduction (see Fig. 1). The importance of detecting fungal and mycotoxin contamination. Fungi are among the most resilient spoilage microorganisms and can be missed by the most advanced control safety measures utilized in the food industry. Fungi are rapidly dispersed by water and air, can survive under extremely demanding conditions such as thermal, detergent- and preservatives-based processing regimes used in modern farm-to-fork production. Approximately 25% of the global food supply is wasted or lost because of postharvest fungal spoilage. In a survey of food manufacturers in the United States and Europe, 94% of the surveyed producers reported fungal spoilage in the finished product as a frequent problem. The majority of cases were related to nuts (70%), followed by fruits and vegetables (12%), herbs and spices (8%) and cereals (4%). The main food spoilage fungi secrete toxic secondary metabolites, so-called mycotoxins of which the immunosuppressive deoxynivalenol and the carcinogenic aflatoxins are among the most prevalent. The European Commission pointed out that 5–10% of global crops produced are lost annually due to mycotoxin contamination. Considering that the EU produces about 133 million tons of wheat annually, about €1.2-2.4 billion can be lost for wheat alone. Mycotoxins are the most frequently notified food safety hazard in the EU. During the last five years, the number of mycotoxin notifications has been increasing at an average rate of 15% per year, thus highlighting the severity of the problem. During this five-year period, the EU’s Rapid Alert System of Food and Feed (RASFF) reported more than 300 alerts relating to aflatoxin levels exceeding legal limits in peanuts imported into the EU. Moreover, fungal and fungal-like oomycetes (water mould) contaminations constitute an increasingly urgent problem in aquaponics and vertical farming, rapidly developing novel plant and vegetable production systems. Characteristic to aquaponic and hydroponic plant culture is the continuous presence of water in the production system, where the humid or aquatic environment suits most fungal and oomycetes plant pathogens. Strikingly, our partner, the aquaponic company BIGH reported direct losses of aromatic herbs of around 5000 pots. BIGH estimates that it could increase the production by an additional 20,000 pots if they were not forced to reduce their production density due to contamination by oomycetes (downy mildew). Currently, mycotoxins and fungal detection is addressed by laborious reference analytical methods, whereby for fungal detection methods based on culturing with subsequent genetic analysis are applied. Mycotoxins are usually detected via sophisticated lab-based LC-MS/MS-based multi-metabolite methods. Immunoassays (ELISA) are also applied for the rapid determination of a few regulated mycotoxins, especially in industrial settings. However, ELISA protocols still require several sample preparation steps, are not flexible, can hardly be used on-site, and are still prone to matrix effects due to cross reactivities of the employed antibodies. Among the more rapid methods, lateral flow strips could be used even on sites but their price is ill far away from being optimal. The relevant limit of detection (LOD) for mycotoxins will vary depending on the type of mycotoxin, for example in the case of deoxynivalenol (DON) the LOD is around 1000 ppb. Therefore, prevention and control of these toxic secondary metabolites remains difficult, and the agriculture and food industries continue to be vulnerable to problems of contamination. The importance of detecting pesticide residuals in cereals, vegetables and fruits. Pesticides are used in agriculture to satisfy a continuously increasing consumer demand for agricultural products by minimizing the pre- and post-harvest losses. While agriculture productivity is increasing, pesticides represent hazards to consumers since their residues accumulate in different fractions in agricultural products such as cereals, vegetables and fruits. The persistence of pesticides has been reported for wheat products, where milling distributes pesticide residues in different fractions in the bran, germ, and endosperm. In addition, pesticide residues could be detected in flower and in bread. In general, food processing allows decrease in pesticide levels. However, in some cases, residue levels of pesticides even increased in the final product due to the processing of the raw materials. This can be related to water removal, for example in the production of dry fruits such as raisins, prunes and apricots. The control of pesticide residues in such novel productions as aquaponics has a particular importance, since pesticides and herbicides are toxic for fish. Today, the detection of pesticide residues is time-consuming and performed by the traditional instrumental chemistry techniques, such as gas chromatography (GC), liquid chromatography (LC) or capillary electrophoresis (CE), coupled to different detectors, especially to mass spectrometers (MS). While several biosensors were developed for the quick detection of pesticides, none of them have been accredited for lab use and they are not broadly available on the market. The main reason for that is that the relevant detection limit for pesticides is in 0.1 µkg/g which is not achievable by novel biosensors. The importance of detecting antibiotics in aquaponics. Aquaponics integrates recirculating aquaculture producing fish and hydroponics producing plants. In standard aquaculture, antibiotics are regularly used for fish production in order to eliminate the risk of fish disease. However, antibiotics are not allowed in aquaponics because they may impact plant growth, as well as quality and the maintenance of nutrient solubilizing

1.1 Objectives  
Due to our unique properties, lightweight and versatility, plastics have outgrown most man-made materials. They are especially used throughout the agri-food value chain (the waste thereof will be referred to in our proposal as AWP: agri-food waste plastics1). From the globally produced 350 Mtons (million metric tons) of plastics per year, our target sectors consume in the range of 50% (packaging 40%, agriculture 3.5%)2 and, in fact, they represent even a greater share of single and short-term use plastics. Nevertheless, the challenges linked to their end-of-life have long been under environmental scrutiny. Indeed, only 31% of plastic is currently recycled and an excessive 30-40% is landfilled, this being due to infra-developed technologies or to their unsatisfactory economic viability 3. In addition, according to the Ellen MacArthur Foundation 4, as much as 32% end up out of controlled waste management systems and leak into our environment.  
Plastic durability is a further challenge for agricultural films, whose collection for materials recycling at end of life is broadly jeopardized due to their fouling. The affordable biodegradable plastic alternatives cannot degrade in the soil but could be sent to composting plants, adding a significant economic hurdle to their greater material cost and lower performance than mostly used fossil counterparts. As of 2017, approximately 6,300 Mtons of agricultural films plastic waste had been generated, around 9% of which had been recycled, 12% was incinerated, and 79% was accumulated in agri-fields (buried), landfills or the natural environment.  
Likewise, more than half of the used food packaging collected in municipal wastes cannot be easily recycled due to the presence of leftover products and the fact that, as for certain mulching films and for most food packaging, they contain complex laminated materials preventing their reprocessing. As such, most of the flexible and/or multilayer packaging is currently incinerated or even landfilled. This amounts to in the range of 13 Mtons per year in Europe5.  
Our project, RECOVER, is a disruptive RIA proposal built upon the actual needs to solve the contamination of agro-fields with non-biodegradable agro-plastics from our end users as well as to improve municipal waste handling by drastically decreasing the packaging fraction going to unsustainable waste management routes.  
We intend to reach our ambitious objectives by applying biotech solutions that will include symbiotic bioprocesses between previously reported high-powered microorganisms through microbiotic systems vectored by several insect species. Indeed, one of the main innovations in our proposal is the combination of endogenous and exogenous microorganisms and their inoculation on insects. This will allow us to achieve higher levels of biodegradation and direct conversion (at the same time) of AWP into insect by-products such as chitin-chitosan (high value as bioplastics raw materials and other interesting industrial applications).  
A great attention will be dedicated to establishing simple and reproducible environmental conditions that can be replicated in our farms or municipalities as a profitable activity that can benefit the whole value chains from plastic producers to the end-users involved in our project. As such, the business model, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), Life Cycle Cost (LCC) and Technology Advanced Analysis (TEA) will be studied throughout our project. To validate the new metabolic routes and biotech tools, further certifications will be carried out regarding the actual biodegradation reached and innovative tools will be developed to support the implementation of the new process on suitable pre-sorted plastics fractions.  
It is estimated that tapping upon a share of the yearly used agricultural films and non-recyclable food packaging (43% of plastics consumed in the EU), the newly developed organic recycling route will help increase plastic recycling in the EU by 12% (details in section 2), generating ~3,460 new jobs and an annual turnover of €724 million by 2030 (details specified in section 2). Furthermore, it will reduce CO2 emissions derived from the AWP previously incinerated. The proposed bioremediation approach could be further expanded to recover plastic in existing landfills and highly polluted areas.  
All in all, whereas there are huge challenges with the currently available end-of-life treatment of the plastics used in the agriculture and food industries, our project, RECOVER, will help provide novel biotechnological solutions applying microorganisms, novel enzymes, earthworms and insects to degrade conventional plastic packaging and agricultural films waste streams but also result in new feedstocks for the bio-based industries. The process will be optimized and performed either ex-situ in insect rearing chambers and composting reactors (preferred route for non-recyclable plastics from municipal solid waste or for agricultural films that can be collected easily) or in-situ in the case of bioremediating soil pollution, by e.g. mulching films, further contributing to the long-term removal of non-biodegradable polymers from the environment. In the former case, our project will also deliver chitin/chitosan extracted from the vectorized insects after their use, which can in turn be a new source of bioplastics used in our target sectors for e.g. improved mulching films and active packaging, as well as in new fertilizers valorizing in cascade any by-products of the process. In addition, RECOVER can solve the microplastics pollution at the level of both industrial composting and soil.  
To achieve our goals, we have defined a 48-month work plan with the following specific objectives, linked to their specific WPs and performance indicators (used as milestones for project implementation):  
 
Objective 1. To quantify and characterize AWP in order to select the proper ones for our project and define feasible logistics for collection, sorting and pre-treatment (Linked to WP1)  
We will make a comprehensive inventory and report on plastic types, collection strategies, cost, quality, safety and sustainability of AWP collection and possible pre-treatment. All partners will be involved in this task and the collected data will constitute key elements to set the further approaches to treat AWP and our RECOVER business strategies. KPI1: by month 6 (MS1), a selection of 4-6 types of most representative fossil plastics (including in each case virgin plastics and AWP counterparts) picked individually from MSW or agricultural films will be available in order to provide normalized samples to all the WPs. By month 12 (MS2), we will obtain the matrix for the definition of the classification, characterization and recommended biodegradation procedure. By month 12 (MS3), we will define the abiotic pre-treatment to be used depending on the mixture of AWP to biodegrade, and of micro-macro organisms suitable for AWP biodegradation.  
 
Objective 2. To select microbial communities, novel synthetic enzymes, earthworms and insects for AWP biodegradation (Linked to WP2)  
We will identify strains with greater capacity to use AWP as a carbon source and explore the coding space of enzyme families in a focused fashion to combine independently evolved enzyme optimizations from different lineages of the same species, and from independent lineages that would have no capabilities to recombine and co-evolve enzymes in Nature. At least 4-6 species of insects and 2 species of earthworms for biodegradation of AWP will be tested. KPI2: by month 12 (MS4), we will have 3-4 microbial consortia and 2-3 enzymes selected. By month 18 (MS5), we will have 2-3 microbial/enzyme pools for each earthworm and insect selected. By month 24 (MS6), novel enzymes and new strategies for directed evolution will be available.  
 
Objective 3. To upscale the production of the suitable biotechnological solutions and combine them (Linked to WP3)  
We will produce stable plastic-degrading microbial/enzyme consortia (PMC) and worms and insects/larvae fortified with PMC for enhanced AWP degradation capability. KPI3: by month 24, at least 2-4 stable microbial/enzyme inoculums (MS7) and 1-2 fortified insects and earthworms (MS8) will be available for the upscaling AWP biodegradation trials in WP4.  
 
Objective 4. To upscale and monitor biodegradation capacities of the fortified microbial consortia (FMC) (Linked to WP4)  
We will test the inoculum from the previous WP by adding it in soil as bioremediation to AWP pollution as well as to feed earthworms/insects larvae, permitting them to improve AWP biodegradation in bioreactors. Monitoring and modeling strategies will be put in place to optimize the process. KPI4: by month 30 (MS9), we will define process conditions for enhanced AWP biodegradation (MS9). By month 36, we will have upscaled ex-situ and in-situ AWP biodegradation processes performed (MS10) and biodegradation modeling established (MS11).  
 
Objective 5. To develop added value downstream products (Linked to WP5)  
We will set up a green process to extract chitosan from the waste insects involved in the AWP degradation. The valorization of chitin and derived chitosan will be tested as an additive in active plastic for packaging and mulching films providing anti-microbial and self-healing properties. Biofertilizer from vermicompost, insect manure and residues from chitosan extraction will be tested. KPI5: by month 36 (MS12), we will have 2-3 fertilizer products based on vermicompost, insect and/or chitin available. By month 42 (MS13), we will define the extraction process for chitin from our REC
